title: AT&T loses key ruling in bid to stop offering basic phone service in California
gist: A federal judge denied AT&T's preliminary injunction against California's Carrier of Last Resort rules, keeping the carrier obligated to offer basic phone service statewide—California being the only one of AT&T's 21 wireline states where it still carries COLR duties AT&T claims cost $1 billion a year to maintain a century-old copper network.

insights.claim: AT&T alleges California's COLR rules force it to spend $1 billion each year to maintain a century-old telephone network that almost no one uses.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: The $1B annual carrying cost quantifies the stranded-asset drag on AT&T's California wireline segment that regulation prevents shedding.
insights.quote: California requires AT&T to spend $1 billion each year to maintain a century-old telephone network that almost no one uses.
insights.locator: California suggests AT&T upgrade old lines to fiber

insights.claim: AT&T has obtained relief from COLR obligations in 20 of the 21 states in its wireline territory, with California the sole holdout.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: California is the lone regulatory blocker to AT&T's copper-retirement strategy—the single-state fight that gates the cost savings.
insights.quote: it has received relief from COLR obligations in 20 of the 21 states in its wireline service territory, all except California.
insights.locator: AT&T has said it has received relief

insights.claim: AT&T wants to stop serving new customers immediately and discontinue service for 184,000 residential and 15,000 business customers on June 1, 2027, without promising to replace copper lines with fiber where it deems installation unprofitable.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Quantifies the customer base AT&T seeks to shed; the no-fiber-guarantee is the crux regulators are resisting.
insights.locator: AT&T wants to stop offering basic phone service to new customers

insights.claim: US District Judge Linda Lopez denied AT&T's preliminary injunction, meaning AT&T remains under California's orders to keep offering phone service while the case continues.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: The injunction denial signals the court views AT&T as unlikely to win on the merits—raising the odds copper obligations persist near-term.
insights.locator: US District Judge Linda Lopez denied AT&T’s request

insights.claim: California argues AT&T's 'analog versus digital' framing is 'window dressing' to escape COLR obligations entirely, noting AT&T already offers basic service over fiber in some locations and its rules are technology-neutral.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: The state's reframing—that no federal-state conflict exists—is the argument that defeated the injunction and could win the case.
insights.quote: AT&T is using its “analog versus digital” narrative as more palatable window dressing for the relief that it actually wants

insights.claim: AT&T is pursuing a parallel path at the FCC, asking for an order specifically preempting California's COLR mandates, though the FCC has not yet acted and California could still challenge any such order in court.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: The FCC preemption request is AT&T's alternate route around the courts—a regulatory catalyst to monitor even if litigation stalls.
insights.locator: AT&T seeking more help from FCC

insights.claim: California says AT&T has never formally sought to substitute copper POTS with its wireless AT&T Phone-Advanced service and does not claim it will offer basic service through it—undercutting AT&T's replacement narrative.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: AT&T seeking full relief rather than approval of a wireless substitute weakens its public-interest case—key evidentiary weakness.
insights.quote: AT&T “does not want to meet its COLR obligations through AP-A; it seeks relief from meeting them at all,”
insights.locator: While AT&T says its wireless AT&T Phone-Advanced

insights.claim: Advocates argue wireless home phone is ill-suited for California's terrain and weather, since AT&T Phone-Advanced carries only a 24-hour backup battery while wildfire and earthquake power outages can last days or weeks.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Reliability gaps (24-hour battery vs multi-week outages) give regulators a durable public-safety rationale to keep copper mandates.
insights.quote: AP-A comes with only a 24-hour backup battery
